source.journal: Proceedings of the National Academy of Sciences of the United States of America

claim_chain: Observational studies are vulnerable to genetic confounding
Existing methods rely on polygenic scores or GWAS summary statistics which have limitations
We develop a method using genetic relatedness matrix to control genetic confounding
Our method outperforms existing approaches especially at moderate sample sizes
The method is robust to GWAS portability concerns
Application to UK Biobank reveals cohort-specific effects of loneliness on health outcomes

typical_pattern: Connect to prior work or theoretical framework
Elaborate on implications or technical details
Point to future directions or broader significance

likely_priorities: Computational tractability and scalability to large sample sizes
Robustness of GRM construction assumptions across different generative models
Clarity about causal model and interpretation of exposure effects
Comparison with existing methods under various conditions
likely_attacks: The canonical GRM may not be optimal and assumes specific effect size-allele frequency relationships
Many alternative data-generating mechanisms remain unexplored in simulations
GCTA-GREML becomes computationally expensive at large sample sizes
Different causal models require different interpretations of the exposure effect
The method requires individual-level genotype data which may not always be available
reviewer_preference_profile.inference_basis: The discussion explicitly addresses computational tractability limitations, stating that GCTA-GREML becomes increasingly slow and computationally expensive as sample sizes increase into the tens of thousands. The authors acknowledge that there are different ways to construct GRMs and that the canonical GRM is not necessarily optimal, noting it corresponds to implicit assumptions about effect sizes. They state that the performance of the GRM under different generative models remains to be characterized and that many alternative data-generating mechanisms remain unexplored. The paper also discusses alternative causal models and notes that different models require different interpretations. These self-identified limitations form the basis for inferring likely reviewer concerns.

the canonical GRM generally provides adequate control of genetic confounding, even when the data are generated under alternative models

verification_status.notes: All extracted phrases are literal substrings from the input text. No external references, author names, or invented numbers were included. The profile is based entirely on the provided discussion, abstract, and figure legends.
